title: Slab-built plant pot saucer set
subtitle: Three matched shallow saucers in small, medium, and large sizes
excerpt: Three matching saucers formed by pressing rolled clay slabs over upturned bowls, giving a gentle curved profile. No kiln is needed. PVA sealing prepares the set for a windowsill plant collection.
sourceNotes: Technique derived from first principles. Encyclopedia Britannica 11th edition (1911) pottery entry consulted for historical context on slab-building methods.

glossaryTerms.definition: A flat sheet of clay rolled to an even thickness, used as the primary building unit in slab hand-building
The stage at which clay has dried enough to hold its shape but remains damp enough to be trimmed or smoothed without crumbling
The stage at which all moisture has left the clay and the piece is ready for surface treatment
Unfired clay work that has dried fully but is fragile and soluble in water until sealed
Shaping a clay slab by laying it over or pressing it into a curved mould so gravity produces a consistent curved profile

content.text: This project produces three matching saucers for plant pots: small (8 cm diameter), medium (12 cm), and large (16 cm). Each saucer is made by the
drape-forming
method, pressing a rolled clay
slab
over an upturned household bowl or plate. No kiln is required. PVA sealing prepares the pieces for indoor use.
Preparation

Select three rounded household bowls or plates that match your target diameters when turned upside down: approximately 8 cm, 12 cm, and 16 cm across the base. Cover each mould with a sheet of cling film before draping the clay over it. The cling film allows the clay to release cleanly without sticking to the mould surface. Knead each portion of clay for two minutes before rolling to eliminate air pockets. Work on a wooden board lightly dusted with cornflour.
Construction

Roll the clay to a consistent 5 mm thickness across the entire sheet. Use two wooden guides of equal height on either side of the rolling pin to maintain an even thickness as you roll. Roll from the centre outward, rotating the slab a quarter turn between passes.

Cut the three saucer circles. For the small saucer, cut a circle 10 cm in diameter (2 cm larger than the finished saucer to allow for the curve). For the medium, cut 14 cm; for the large, cut 18 cm. Use the fettling knife and a cardboard circle template, or cut around an appropriately sized lid or plate.

Drape each circle over its upturned mould. Lay the clay circle centrally over the cling-filmed mould and press it gently downward with your palm so it conforms to the curve. Smooth the surface with a damp sponge using circular strokes. Check that the clay drapes evenly all around with no creases. If a crease forms, lift that section of clay, smooth it flat, and re-drape.

Trim the rim. Once the saucer is draped and smoothed, use the fettling knife to trim around the edge to create a clean, level rim. Cut at a 90-degree angle to the surface for a straight rim, or at a slight angle for a chamfered finish. Smooth the cut edge with a damp finger or sponge.
Leave the saucers on the moulds until they reach the
leather-hard
stage. At this point they will hold their curved shape when lifted off the mould. Carefully peel away the cling film and place the saucers, curved side up, on a flat wooden board to finish drying.

Check that each saucer sits level. Set each piece on a flat surface. If one side rocks, it dried slightly unevenly on the mould. At the leather-hard stage this can be corrected by pressing the higher side down gently. If it has stiffened too much to adjust, add a thin coil of clay to the lower side of the base to act as a foot ring: score and apply slip to both surfaces before pressing the coil in place.
Add surface decoration at the leather-hard stage if desired. Use the needle tool to incise a border line 10 mm from the rim, or press a stamp into the outer edge for a repeating pattern.
Drying

Allow the saucers to dry at room temperature away from direct heat. The leather-hard stage takes 12-24 hours depending on the ambient humidity. Once removed from the mould and placed curved-side up, allow a further 24-2 days until the clay reaches the
bone-dry
stage: uniformly pale in colour and room temperature to the touch. At the
greenware
stage the pieces are fragile. Stack them only once they are fully bone-dry and only with soft padding between them.
Finishing

Paint all three saucers with acrylic paint if desired and allow to dry for at least two hours. Then apply two coats of PVA glue diluted 50:50 with water, covering the inside surface, outside surface, and rim. Allow each coat to dry before applying the next. This sealing layer slows moisture absorption significantly, but the saucers remain unsuitable for standing in water indefinitely.
Care

Air-dry clay is not waterproof even when PVA-sealed. These saucers are not suitable as water vessels and should not be left with standing water in them for extended periods. Place pot-and-saucer combinations indoors on a windowsill; do not use outdoors in rain. Wipe clean with a barely damp cloth. Do not submerge, soak, or put in a dishwasher.
Variations
Square saucers: cut the slab into squares rather than circles and drape over a square-based mould. Ease the corners down to produce a gently squared form with rounded angles.
Rim texture: press a length of twisted rope or a textured fabric into the outer rim before trimming. The impression sets as the clay dries and gives a tactile border around each saucer.

Terracotta-effect finish: paint the saucers with a terracotta-coloured acrylic and allow to dry. Then dry-brush a lighter sand colour over the surface to pick out raised texture. Seal with PVA when done.

rows.cause: Cling film was omitted or developed a gap
rows.fix: At the leather-hard stage, slide a thin palette knife or fettling knife gently between the clay and the mould to break the suction, then peel away carefully

rows.problem: Cracking along the rim during drying
rows.cause: The rim dried faster than the body, creating tension
rows.fix: During drying, drape a strip of damp cloth lightly over the rim to slow that zone. At the leather-hard stage, work a little slip into any crack and smooth over

rows.problem: Saucer warps and does not sit flat
rows.cause: Uneven drying, often from direct sunlight on one side
rows.fix: At leather-hard, press the piece gently flat on the board and leave weighted under another board. If fully dry and warped, use a small clay foot pad on the high side to level it

rows.cause: Clay was too stiff or rolled too thin
rows.fix: Re-knead the clay and roll to 5 mm minimum. If a tear occurs, smooth the edges together with a damp finger while the clay is still soft
